1: A type: "Hear my prayer, O LORD; let my cry come to you! Do not hide your face from me in the day of my distress!" The prayer of the one who is "faint" and "pours out his complaint." Hebrews 5:7 — Christ "offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The prayer of the afflicted that Ps 102 rehearses is the prayer Christ prayed in Gethsemane.
2: A type: "Incline your ear to me; answer me speedily in the day when I call!" The urgency of the sufferer's cry. Luke 22:44 — "being in agony he prayed more earnestly." Hebrews 5:7 — "he was heard." The Father answered the cry not with exemption from death but with resurrection — the answer that surpassed the prayer by giving not just relief but eternal life.
3: A type: "For my days pass away like smoke, and my bones burn like a furnace." The physical wasting of the afflicted one. Isaiah 53:2-3 — "he had no form or majesty that we should look at him... he was despised and rejected by men; a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief." Christ assumed fully the suffering and transience of the afflicted human being — bone, breath, body — to redeem it from within.
4: A type: "My heart is struck down like grass and has withered; I forget to eat my bread." The collapse of body and will under extremity. Luke 22:43 — an angel appeared to strengthen him. John 19:28 — "I thirst" — even the basic need for water abandoned. Christ descended into the complete human experience of extremity so that the extreme sufferer would find him there.
5: A type: "Because of my loud groaning my bones cling to my skin." Matthew 27:50 — "Jesus cried out again with a loud voice and yielded up his spirit." Hebrews 5:7 — "with loud cries and tears." The loud groaning of the psalmist is the loud cry of Christ from the cross — the extremity of suffering expressed in the only language available: the wordless cry.
6: A type: "I am like a desert owl of the wilderness, like an owl of the waste places." The imagery of lonely, nocturnal creatures in desolate places. Gethsemane — Jesus "withdrew from them about a stone's throw, and knelt down and prayed" (Luke 22:41). John 16:32 — "you will leave me alone." The desert owl alone in the waste places is the type of Christ's lonely prayer in the garden and his abandonment on the cross.
7: A type: "I lie awake; I am like a lonely sparrow on the housetop." The sleepless, solitary vigil. Luke 22:39-46 — Christ prayed through the night in Gethsemane while the disciples slept (Luke 22:45 — "he found them sleeping for sorrow"). He was the solitary watcher, the lonely sparrow, while those around him could not keep watch for even one hour (Mark 14:37).
8: A type: "All the day my enemies taunt me; those who deride me use my name for a curse." Matthew 27:39-44 — "those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads and saying, 'You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself!'... the chief priests also, along with the scribes and elders, mocked him." Galatians 3:13 — "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." He became a curse so we could receive the blessing.
9: A type: "For I eat ashes like bread and mingle tears with my drink." John 11:35 — "Jesus wept." Luke 19:41 — "he wept over it (Jerusalem)." Hebrews 5:7 — "with loud cries and tears." The one who ate ashes and mingled tears with his drink offered at the Last Supper the new wine of the covenant — "this cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood" (Luke 22:20). His tears became the cup of salvation.
10: A type: "because of your indignation and anger; for you have taken me up and thrown me down." The divine wrath falling on the righteous sufferer. Isaiah 53:10 — "it was the will of the LORD to crush him." Galatians 3:13 — Christ became a curse for us. Romans 3:25 — God presented him as a propitiation. The divine indignation that falls on the psalmist is the divine judgment that fell on Christ vicariously — he was "taken up and thrown down" so that we would be raised up with him (Ephesians 2:6).
11: A type: "My days are like an evening shadow; I wither away like grass." Philippians 2:7-8 — Christ "made himself nothing... becoming obedient to the point of death." He who is "the same yesterday and today and forever" (Hebrews 13:8) entered the withering and the shadow of mortality — the eternal Son took on the transience of flesh so that the transient could be clothed with eternity (2 Corinthians 5:4).
12: A revelation of God: "But you, O LORD, are enthroned forever; you are remembered throughout all generations." The contrast between the wasting psalmist and the eternal God. This contrast becomes the paradox of Christ — the eternal Son who wasted away in death (Hebrews 13:8 — "the same yesterday, today, and forever") rose and reigns forever. The eternally enthroned one is the one who died and rose (Revelation 1:18 — "I am alive forevermore").
13: A shadow: "You will arise and have pity on Zion; it is the time to favor her; the appointed time has come." Galatians 4:4 — "when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law." 2 Corinthians 6:2 — "Behold, now is the favorable time; behold, now is the day of salvation." The "appointed time" that Ps 102 awaits is the time that Paul says has arrived in Christ — the fullness of time, the acceptable year of the Lord.
14: A theme: devotion to Zion's stones and dust — the longing for Jerusalem's restoration. This longing is fulfilled in the heavenly Jerusalem. Hebrews 11:10 — Abraham "was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God." Revelation 21:2 — "the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God." The devotion to Zion's dust is the devotion to the eternal city that Christ is building (Matthew 16:18).
15: A shadow: "Nations will fear the name of the LORD, and all the kings of the earth will fear your glory." Revelation 15:3-4 — "who will not fear, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy. All nations will come and worship you." Philippians 2:10-11 — every knee bowing before Christ. The universal fear of God's name among the nations is the universal acknowledgment of Christ's lordship that the gospel is bringing about.
16: A shadow: "For the LORD builds up Zion; he appears in his glory." Matthew 16:18 — "I will build my church." Hebrews 12:22 — the heavenly Zion. Matthew 25:31 — "when the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him." The LORD who builds Zion and appears in glory is Christ who builds his church and will appear at the second coming. The building and the appearing belong together.
17: A shadow: "he regards the prayer of the destitute and does not despise their prayer." Hebrews 4:15-16 — Christ "sympathizes with our weaknesses," so "let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need." He who prayed as the destitute one (Psalms 102:1-11) is now the one who hears the destitute and does not despise their cry. The sufferer becomes the sympathetic high priest.
18: A shadow: "Let this be recorded for a generation to come, so that a people yet to be created may praise the LORD." 1 Peter 2:9-10 — "you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession, that you may proclaim the excellencies of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light. Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people." The "people yet to be created" are the born-again community of Christ — the new humanity that the gospel creates.
19: A type: "that he looked down from his holy height; from heaven the LORD looked at the earth, to hear the groans of the prisoners." John 3:13 — "no one has ascended into heaven except he who descended from heaven, the Son of Man." John 3:16-17 — "God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son." The God who looks down from heaven and descends to hear the prisoners is the God who sends and is the Son, who descends to liberate those "doomed to die."
20: A type: "to set free those who were doomed to die." Luke 4:18 — "he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives." Hebrews 2:14-15 — Christ through death destroyed "the one who has the power of death... and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery." Those "doomed to die" are liberated through Christ's own death — the substitution that sets the condemned free.
21: A shadow: "that they may declare in Zion the name of the LORD, and in Jerusalem his praise." Acts 2 — Pentecost: people from every nation declaring the mighty works of God in Zion/Jerusalem (Acts 2:5-11). Philippians 2:10-11 — every tongue confessing the name of Jesus. The declaration of God's name in Zion is fulfilled at Pentecost and completed at the final gathering (Revelation 7:9-10).
22: A shadow: "when peoples gather together, and kingdoms, to worship the LORD." Revelation 7:9-10 — "a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb, crying out with a loud voice." The universal gathering of peoples and kingdoms to worship is the eschatological assembly before the throne of God and the Lamb.
23: A type: "He has broken my strength in the way; he has shortened my days." Christ was crucified in his early thirties. Isaiah 53:8 — "by oppression and judgment he was taken away... he was cut off out of the land of the living." The one "broken in the way" and whose days were shortened is the one whose death at the height of his ministry was the hinge of all history — his cutting off is the ground of his people's life (Isaiah 53:8-10).
24: A type: "O my God, do not take me away in the midst of my days!" The prayer not to die prematurely — the prayer that Christ prayed in its own form in Gethsemane (Luke 22:42 — "Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me"). He died in the midst of his days — and yet "it was not possible for him to be held by death" (Acts 2:24). He died in the midst of his days but lives for all days.
25: A direct: "Of old you laid the foundation of the earth, and the heavens are the work of your hands." Hebrews 1:10 quotes this verse and applies it directly to Christ: "And, 'You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands.'" John 1:3 — "all things were made through him." Colossians 1:16 — "all things were created through him and for him." The Creator addressed in Psalms 102:25 is the Christ addressed in Hebrews 1:10.
26: A direct: "They will perish, but you will remain; they will all wear out like a garment. You will change them like a robe, and they will pass away." Hebrews 1:11-12 continues the direct quotation and applies it to Christ — the eternal one before whom created things pass away. 2 Peter 3:10-13 — "the heavens will pass away with a roar and the heavenly bodies will be burned up and dissolved." The perishable creation stands over against the imperishable Christ.
27: A direct: "but you are the same, and your years have no end." Hebrews 1:12b — "but you are the same, and your years will have no end." Hebrews 13:8 — "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever." The eternal sameness of the one addressed in Psalms 102:27 is the eternal sameness of Jesus Christ, as Hebrews explicitly declares. This is the clearest identification in Hebrews of the God of the Psalms as Jesus Christ.
28: A shadow: "The children of your servants shall dwell secure; their offspring shall be established before you." John 10:28-29 — "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand." 1 Peter 1:5 — "who by God's power are being guarded through faith." Revelation 22:3-4 — "the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in it, and his servants will worship him. They will see his face, and his name will be on their foreheads." The offspring established before God are the redeemed before the throne of Christ.
